library(lterdatasampler)
library(palmerpenguins)


Attaching package: 'palmerpenguins'

The following objects are masked from 'package:datasets':

    penguins, penguins_raw

library(rstatix)


Attaching package: 'rstatix'

The following object is masked from 'package:stats':

    filter

data(and_vertebrates)

title: Statistical Power: P-value Simulation Across Sample Sizes
text:
Background
Filter Cutthroat trout data and run a single 5-sample t-test as a baseline.

trout <- and_vertebrates %>%
  filter(species == "Cutthroat trout") %>%
  drop_na(weight_g)

trout_5 <- trout %>%
  group_by(section) %>%
  slice_sample(n = 5) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  t_test(weight_g ~ section, var.equal = FALSE, detailed = FALSE)

trout_5

# A tibble: 1 × 8
  .y.      group1 group2    n1    n2 statistic    df     p
* <chr>    <chr>  <chr>  <int> <int>     <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>
1 weight_g CC     OG         5     5     0.921  7.42 0.386

title: Statistical Power: P-value Simulation Across Sample Sizes
text:
1 & 2. Sampling function
A function that samples n fish per section and runs a Welch t-test.

trout_subber <- function(n) {
  trout %>%
    group_by(section) %>%
    slice_sample(n = n) %>%
    ungroup() %>%
    t_test(weight_g ~ section, var.equal = FALSE, detailed = FALSE)
}

title: Statistical Power: P-value Simulation Across Sample Sizes
text:
3. Simulate across sample sizes
Run 100 iterations each for n = 5, 10, 1000, and 5000.

sample_sizes <- rep(c(5, 10, 1000, 5000), each = 100)
trout_results <- map_dfr(sample_sizes, trout_subber)

title: Statistical Power: P-value Simulation Across Sample Sizes
text:
4. P-value distributions

trout_results %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = p)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "blue", color = "black") +
  facet_wrap(~ n1, labeller = label_both) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0.05, color = "red", linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 1) +
  labs(
    x = "P-value",
    y = "Count",
    title = "Distribution of P-values Across Different Sample Sizes"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

Attaching package: 'rstatix'

The following object is masked from 'package:stats':

    filter

library(lubridate)

data("ntl_airtemp")
data("ntl_icecover")

title: Linear Regression: Lake Ice Duration and Winter Temperature
text:
Data Preparation
Calculate mean ice duration by water year and mean winter air temperature (Nov–Apr) by water year.

avg_icecover <- ntl_icecover %>%
  group_by(wyear = year + 1) %>%
  summarize(mean_duration = mean(ice_duration, na.rm = TRUE))

ntl_airtemp_wyear <- ntl_airtemp %>%
  mutate(wyear = if_else(month(sampledate) < 10, year, year + 1))

ntl_winter_airtemp <- ntl_airtemp_wyear %>%
  filter(lubridate::month(sampledate) %in% c(11, 12, 1:4)) %>%
  group_by(wyear) %>%
  summarize(mean_air_temp = mean(ave_air_temp_adjusted))

title: Linear Regression: Lake Ice Duration and Winter Temperature
text:
1. Join datasets

icecover_temp <- avg_icecover %>%
  inner_join(ntl_winter_airtemp, by = "wyear")

title: Linear Regression: Lake Ice Duration and Winter Temperature
text:
2. Exploratory plot

ggplot(data = icecover_temp, aes(x = mean_air_temp, y = mean_duration)) +
  geom_point() +
  labs(
    x = "Mean Winter Air Temperature (°C)",
    y = "Mean Ice Duration (days)",
    title = "Relationship between Winter Air Temperature and Ice Duration"
  ) +
  theme_bw()

title: Linear Regression: Lake Ice Duration and Winter Temperature
text:
3. Correlation test

shapiro.test(icecover_temp$mean_air_temp)


    Shapiro-Wilk normality test

data:  icecover_temp$mean_air_temp
W = 0.98939, p-value = 0.3073

shapiro.test(icecover_temp$mean_duration)


    Shapiro-Wilk normality test

data:  icecover_temp$mean_duration
W = 0.97623, p-value = 0.009812

cor_test(icecover_temp,
         vars = c(mean_air_temp, mean_duration),
         alternative = "two.sided",
         method = "pearson")

# A tibble: 1 × 8
  var1          var2            cor statistic       p conf.low conf.high method 
  <chr>         <chr>         <dbl>     <dbl>   <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl> <chr>  
1 mean_air_temp mean_duration  -0.8     -16.1 1.4e-34   -0.848    -0.730 Pearson

title: Linear Regression: Lake Ice Duration and Winter Temperature
text:
4. Simple linear regression

ice_temp_model <- lm(mean_duration ~ mean_air_temp, data = icecover_temp)
summary(ice_temp_model)


Call:
lm(formula = mean_duration ~ mean_air_temp, data = icecover_temp)

Residuals:

Residual standard error: 11.43 on 150 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.6343,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.6319 
F-statistic: 260.2 on 1 and 150 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16

predict(ice_temp_model, newdata = new_temps)

        1         2         3 
102.99111  85.18557  67.38003

title: Linear Regression: Lake Ice Duration and Winter Temperature
text:
5. Regression plot

ggplot(data = icecover_temp, aes(x = mean_air_temp, y = mean_duration)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  labs(
    x = "Mean Winter Air Temperature (°C)",
    y = "Mean Ice Duration (days)",
    title = "Relationship between Winter Air Temperature and Ice Duration"
  ) +
  theme_bw()

`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

library(lterdatasampler)
library(rstatix)


Attaching package: 'rstatix'

The following object is masked from 'package:stats':

    filter

data("and_vertebrates")

title: Hypothesis Testing: Independent Samples T-Test
text:
Data Preparation
Filter for Coastal giant salamander and remove missing length measurements.

salamander_data <- and_vertebrates %>%
  filter(species == "Coastal giant salamander") %>%
  select(section, length_1_mm) %>%
  filter(!is.na(length_1_mm), !is.na(section)) %>%
  filter(length_1_mm > 0)

glimpse(salamander_data)

Rows: 11,749
Columns: 2
$ section     <chr> "CC", "CC", "CC", "CC", "CC", "CC", "CC", "CC", "CC", "CC"…
$ length_1_mm <dbl> 72, 85, 61, 42, 61, 81, 55, 70, 69, 25, 46, 88, 100, 85, 5…

salamander_summary <- salamander_data %>%
  group_by(section) %>%
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    mean_length = mean(length_1_mm),
    sd_length = sd(length_1_mm),
    median_length = median(length_1_mm),
    .groups = 'drop'
  )

print(salamander_summary)

# A tibble: 2 × 5
  section     n mean_length sd_length median_length
  <chr>   <int>       <dbl>     <dbl>         <dbl>
1 CC       5397        60.5      20.8            58
2 OG       6352        54.0      20.5            52

title: Hypothesis Testing: Independent Samples T-Test
text:
Assumption Checks

A. Normality
Shapiro-Wilk test: if p > 0.05 the data are approximately normally distributed. Q-Q plots provide a visual check.

sample_sizes <- salamander_data %>% count(section)
print(sample_sizes)

# A tibble: 2 × 2
  section     n
  <chr>   <int>
1 CC       5397
2 OG       6352

tryCatch({
  normality_tests <- salamander_data %>%
    group_by(section) %>%
    shapiro_test(length_1_mm)
  print(normality_tests)
}, error = function(e) {
  for (forest_type in unique(salamander_data$section)) {
    group_data <- salamander_data %>% filter(section == forest_type)
    if (nrow(group_data) >= 3 & nrow(group_data) <= 5000) {
      test_result <- shapiro.test(group_data$length_1_mm)
      cat(sprintf("Forest type: %s, n = %d, W = %.4f, p-value = %.4f\n",
                  forest_type, nrow(group_data), test_result$statistic, test_result$p.value))
    }
  }
})

qq_plot <- salamander_data %>%
  ggplot(aes(sample = length_1_mm)) +
  geom_qq() +
  geom_qq_line() +
  facet_wrap(~section) +
  labs(title = "Q-Q Plots for Normality Check",
       subtitle = "Coastal Giant Salamander Length by Forest Type") +
  theme_minimal()

print(qq_plot)










B. Equal Variances
Levene’s test for homoscedasticity.

variance_test <- salamander_data %>%
  levene_test(length_1_mm ~ section)

Warning in leveneTest.default(y = y, group = group, ...): group coerced to
factor.

print(variance_test)

# A tibble: 1 × 4
    df1   df2 statistic        p
  <int> <int>     <dbl>    <dbl>
1     1 11747      14.3 0.000157

boxplot_check <- salamander_data %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = section, y = length_1_mm)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(title = "Boxplot: Salamander Length by Forest Type",
       subtitle = "Visual check for equal variances and outliers",
       x = "Forest Type (Section)",
       y = "Snout-vent Length (mm)") +
  theme_minimal()

print(boxplot_check)

title: Hypothesis Testing: Independent Samples T-Test
text:
T-Test and Effect Size

ttest_result <- salamander_data %>%
  t_test(length_1_mm ~ section, var.equal = TRUE)

print(ttest_result)

# A tibble: 1 × 8
  .y.         group1 group2    n1    n2 statistic    df        p
* <chr>       <chr>  <chr>  <int> <int>     <dbl> <dbl>    <dbl>
1 length_1_mm CC     OG      5397  6352      17.0 11747 8.75e-64

effect_size <- salamander_data %>%
  cohens_d(length_1_mm ~ section)

print(effect_size)

# A tibble: 1 × 7
  .y.         group1 group2 effsize    n1    n2 magnitude
* <chr>       <chr>  <chr>    <dbl> <int> <int> <ord>    
1 length_1_mm CC     OG       0.314  5397  6352 small

title: Hypothesis Testing: Independent Samples T-Test
text:
Final Plot

clean_boxplot <- salamander_data %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = section, y = length_1_mm, fill = section)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.8, outlier.size = 1.5, outlier.alpha = 0.6) +
  stat_summary(fun = mean, geom = "point", shape = 23, size = 4,
               fill = "white", color = "black", stroke = 1) +
  labs(
    title = "Snout-vent Length of Coastal Giant Salamanders",
    subtitle = "Comparison between forest types",
    x = "Forest Type",
    y = "Snout-vent Length (mm)"
  ) +
  theme_classic() +
  theme(
    legend.position = "none",
    plot.title = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 12),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 11),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 10)
  ) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2")

print(clean_boxplot)

data("penguins")

title: Data Wrangling with dplyr
text:
Question 1
Fixing common errors: c() for multiple columns, correcting spelling, specifying the data frame name.

penguins[1:5, c("species", "island")]

# A tibble: 5 × 2
  species island   
  <fct>   <fct>    
1 Adelie  Torgersen
2 Adelie  Torgersen
3 Adelie  Torgersen
4 Adelie  Torgersen
5 Adelie  Torgersen

title: Data Wrangling with dplyr
text:
Question 2
The species with the largest average flipper length is Gentoo at 217 mm, followed by Chinstrap at 196 mm, and smallest Adelie at 190 mm.

penguins %>%
  group_by(species) %>%
  summarise(flipper_length_avg = mean(flipper_length_mm, na.rm = TRUE))

# A tibble: 3 × 2
  species   flipper_length_avg
  <fct>                  <dbl>
1 Adelie                  190.
2 Chinstrap               196.
3 Gentoo                  217.

title: Data Wrangling with dplyr
text:
Question 3
Adelie penguins are on all three islands, whereas Chinstrap and Gentoo are each on one island.

penguins %>%
  group_by(species, island) %>%
  summarise(n_observations = n())

`summarise()` has grouped output by 'species'. You can override using the
`.groups` argument.


# A tibble: 5 × 3
# Groups:   species [3]
  species   island    n_observations
  <fct>     <fct>              <int>
1 Adelie    Biscoe                44
2 Adelie    Dream                 56
3 Adelie    Torgersen             52
4 Chinstrap Dream                 68
5 Gentoo    Biscoe               124

title: Data Wrangling with dplyr
text:
Question 4
Reorder columns to put year first.

penguins %>%
  select(year, everything())

# A tibble: 344 × 8
    year species island    bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_mm
   <int> <fct>   <fct>              <dbl>         <dbl>             <int>
 1  2007 Adelie  Torgersen           39.1          18.7               181
 2  2007 Adelie  Torgersen           39.5          17.4               186

 4  2007 Adelie  Torgersen           NA            NA                  NA

10  2007 Adelie  Torgersen           42            20.2               190
# ℹ 334 more rows
# ℹ 2 more variables: body_mass_g <int>, sex <fct>

title: Data Wrangling with dplyr
text:
Question 5
Create a size group column based on whether each penguin is above or below the mean body mass.

penguins_fixed <- penguins %>%
  mutate(size_group = if_else(
    body_mass_g > mean(penguins$body_mass_g, na.rm = TRUE),
    paste0(species, " large"),
    paste0(species, " small")
  ))
penguins_fixed %>% select(size_group)

# A tibble: 344 × 1
   size_group  
   <chr>       
 1 Adelie small
 2 Adelie small
 3 Adelie small
 4 <NA>        
 5 Adelie small
 6 Adelie small
 7 Adelie small
 8 Adelie large
 9 Adelie small
10 Adelie large
# ℹ 334 more rows

title: Data Wrangling with dplyr
text:
Question 6
Hypothesis: larger penguins cluster by island, suggesting one island has better food resources.

ggplot(penguins) +
  geom_point(mapping = aes(x = body_mass_g, y = flipper_length_mm, color = island))

Warning: Removed 2 rows containing missing values or values outside the scale range
(`geom_point()`).









Biscoe Island has both the largest overall body masses. Checking the range:

penguins_clean <- penguins %>% filter(!is.na(body_mass_g))

island_stats <- penguins_clean %>%
  group_by(island) %>%
  summarise(
    n_penguins = n(),
    min_body_mass = min(body_mass_g),
    max_body_mass = max(body_mass_g)
  )

island_stats

# A tibble: 3 × 4
  island    n_penguins min_body_mass max_body_mass
  <fct>          <int>         <int>         <int>
1 Biscoe           167          2850          6300
2 Dream            124          2700          4800
3 Torgersen         51          2900          4700

title: Principles of Data Visualization
text:
library(ggplot2)

Warning: package 'ggplot2' was built under R version 4.5.2

library(palmerpenguins)


Attaching package: 'palmerpenguins'


The following objects are masked from 'package:datasets':

    penguins, penguins_raw

library(dplyr)


Attaching package: 'dplyr'


The following objects are masked from 'package:stats':

    filter, lag


The following objects are masked from 'package:base':

    intersect, setdiff, setequal, union

data(penguins)
penguins_clean <- penguins %>%
  filter(!is.na(bill_length_mm), !is.na(bill_depth_mm),
         !is.na(flipper_length_mm), !is.na(body_mass_g))

title: Principles of Data Visualization
text:
library(ggplot2)

Warning: package 'ggplot2' was built under R version 4.5.2

library(palmerpenguins)


Attaching package: 'palmerpenguins'


The following objects are masked from 'package:datasets':

    penguins, penguins_raw

library(dplyr)


Attaching package: 'dplyr'


The following objects are masked from 'package:stats':

    filter, lag


The following objects are masked from 'package:base':

    intersect, setdiff, setequal, union

data(penguins)
penguins_clean <- penguins %>%
  filter(!is.na(bill_length_mm), !is.na(bill_depth_mm),
         !is.na(flipper_length_mm), !is.na(body_mass_g))

title: Principles of Data Visualization
text:
The Good Plot
A well-designed plot: clear title and axis labels, colorblind-friendly palette, appropriate point size, regression lines per species, minimal theme.

good_plot <- penguins_clean %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g, color = species)) +
  geom_point(size = 2.5, alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, linewidth = 1) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c("Adelie" = "#FF6B35", "Chinstrap" = "#004E89", "Gentoo" = "#009639"),
    name = "Species"
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Relationship Between Flipper Length and Body Mass in Palmer Penguins",
    subtitle = "Larger penguins tend to have longer flippers across all species",
    x = "Flipper Length (mm)",
    y = "Body Mass (g)",
    caption = "Data source: Palmer Penguins dataset"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold", hjust = 0),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 11, color = "gray30", hjust = 0),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 11, face = "bold"),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 10),
    legend.title = element_text(size = 11, face = "bold"),
    legend.text = element_text(size = 10),
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.caption = element_text(size = 9, color = "gray50")
  )

print(good_plot)

`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

title: Principles of Data Visualization
text:
The Bad Plot
A deliberately poor plot demonstrating common mistakes: misleading title, clashing colors (not colorblind-friendly), angled axis labels, arbitrary axis limits that cut data, garish background colors.

bad_plot <- penguins_clean %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = bill_length_mm, y = bill_depth_mm, color = sex)) +
  geom_point(size = 8, alpha = 1) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c("female" = "red", "male" = "green"),
    name = "Gender of the Penguin Bird"
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "The best graph in the world",
    x = "The length of a panguin bill in millimeters unit",
    y = "mm bill depth of penguin"
  ) +
  xlim(30, 65) +
  ylim(12, 25) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold", color = "purple"),
    axis.title.x = element_text(size = 14, angle = 45, color = "blue"),
    axis.title.y = element_text(size = 14, angle = 45, color = "orange"),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90, size = 6),
    axis.text.y = element_text(angle = 45, size = 6),
    legend.position = "top",
    legend.title = element_text(size = 8),
    panel.background = element_rect(fill = "yellow"),
    plot.background = element_rect(fill = "lightblue"),
    panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "darkgreen", size = 2),
    panel.grid.minor = element_line(color = "pink", size = 1)
  )

Warning: The `size` argument of `element_line()` is deprecated as of ggplot2 3.4.0.
ℹ Please use the `linewidth` argument instead.

print(bad_plot)

data("and_vertebrates")

title: Hypothesis Testing: One-Way ANOVA
text:
Data Preparation
Filter for Coastal giant salamander across three channel unit types (C = cascade, P = pool, SC = side channel).

salamander_data <- and_vertebrates %>%
  filter(species == "Coastal giant salamander",
         unittype %in% c("C", "P", "SC")) %>%
  select(unittype, length_1_mm) %>%
  filter(!is.na(length_1_mm), !is.na(unittype)) %>%
  mutate(unittype = as.factor(unittype))

title: Hypothesis Testing: One-Way ANOVA
text:
Assumption Checks

salamander_data %>%
  group_by(unittype) %>%
  get_summary_stats(length_1_mm, type = "mean_sd")

# A tibble: 3 × 5
  unittype variable        n  mean    sd
  <fct>    <fct>       <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>
1 C        length_1_mm  7688  58.3  20.8
2 P        length_1_mm  1943  57.3  22.8
3 SC       length_1_mm  1994  51.8  18.0

normality_check <- salamander_data %>%
  group_by(unittype) %>%
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    shapiro_p = if (n >= 3 & n <= 5000) shapiro.test(length_1_mm)$p.value else NA,
    .groups = 'drop'
  )

normality_check

# A tibble: 3 × 3
  unittype     n shapiro_p
  <fct>    <int>     <dbl>
1 C         7688 NA       
2 P         1943  2.40e-33
3 SC        1994  2.15e-29

salamander_data %>%
  levene_test(length_1_mm ~ unittype)

# A tibble: 1 × 4
    df1   df2 statistic        p
  <int> <int>     <dbl>    <dbl>
1     2 11622      20.9 8.52e-10

title: Hypothesis Testing: One-Way ANOVA
text:
ANOVA and Post-Hoc Test

anova_result <- salamander_data %>%
  anova_test(length_1_mm ~ unittype)

anova_result

ANOVA Table (type II tests)

    Effect DFn   DFd      F        p p<.05   ges
1 unittype   2 11622 79.073 7.77e-35     * 0.013

if (anova_result$p < 0.05) {
  posthoc_result <- salamander_data %>%
    tukey_hsd(length_1_mm ~ unittype)
  posthoc_result
}

# A tibble: 3 × 9
  term   group1 group2 null.value estimate conf.low conf.high p.adj p.adj.signif
* <chr>  <chr>  <chr>       <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl>     <dbl> <dbl> <chr>       
1 unitt… C      P               0    -1.04    -2.27     0.198  0.12 ns          
2 unitt… C      SC              0    -6.55    -7.77    -5.32   0    ****        
3 unitt… P      SC              0    -5.51    -7.06    -3.96   0    ****        

eta_squared <- salamander_data %>%
  anova_test(length_1_mm ~ unittype)

eta_squared$ges

[1] 0.013

title: Hypothesis Testing: One-Way ANOVA
text:
Visualization

salamander_data %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = unittype, y = length_1_mm, fill = unittype)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7) +
  stat_summary(fun = mean, geom = "point", shape = 23, size = 3,
               fill = "white", color = "black") +
  labs(
    title = "Salamander Length by Channel Type",
    x = "Channel Type",
    y = "Snout-vent Length (mm)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

title: Introduction to R — Fall 2025
text:
Course assignments from ESS Introduction to R, Fall 2025 at Colorado State University. Topics cover data wrangling, visualization, and statistical inference in R using tidyverse, ggplot2, and related packages.



Topic
Description




Data Wrangling
Palmer penguins: dplyr select, filter, group_by, summarise, mutate


Data Visualization
Principles of good vs. bad ggplot design


T-Test
Salamander length: independent samples t-test


ANOVA
Salamander length by channel type: one-way ANOVA & Tukey HSD


Linear Regression
Lake ice duration vs. winter temperature: correlation and simple regression


Multiple Regression
USGS gage streamflow: multiple linear regression


Statistical Power
Cutthroat trout: p-value simulation across sample sizes

title: Multiple Linear Regression: USGS Gage Streamflow
text:
1. Exploratory: Predictors vs. Annual Streamflow

usgs_long <- usgs_data %>%
  select(annual_streamflow_mm, mean_baseflow, ws_area_sqkm, site_elevation,
         median_ws_elevation, mean_annual_precip, mean_winter_temp,
         mean_aridity_index, soil_sand_content, mean_soil_organic_matter,
         mean_soil_permeability, percent_urban, percent_forested,
         percent_wetland, percent_s_facing) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = -annual_streamflow_mm)

ggplot(data = usgs_long, aes(x = value, y = annual_streamflow_mm)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, color = "blue") +
  facet_wrap(~name, scales = "free_x") +
  labs(
    x = "Predictor Variable Value",
    y = "Annual Streamflow (mm)",
    title = "Relationship between Predictors and Annual Streamflow"
  ) +
  theme_bw()

`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

title: Multiple Linear Regression: USGS Gage Streamflow
text:
2. Multiple Linear Regression Model

streamflow_model <- lm(annual_streamflow_mm ~ mean_annual_precip +
                                               ws_area_sqkm +
                                               mean_baseflow +
                                               percent_forested +
                                               mean_aridity_index +
                                               soil_sand_content,
                       data = usgs_data)

summary(streamflow_model)


Call:
lm(formula = annual_streamflow_mm ~ mean_annual_precip + ws_area_sqkm + 
    mean_baseflow + percent_forested + mean_aridity_index + soil_sand_content, 
    data = usgs_data)

Residuals:
     Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
-208.033  -40.807   -3.312   38.155  217.475 

Coefficients:
                     Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)        -789.62214   45.88054 -17.210  < 2e-16 ***
mean_annual_precip    0.33214    0.05126   6.479 1.35e-09 ***

title: Multiple Linear Regression: USGS Gage Streamflow
text:
3. Assumption Diagnostics

plot(streamflow_model, which = 1)







plot(streamflow_model, which = 2)

  select(mean_annual_precip, ws_area_sqkm, mean_baseflow,
         percent_forested, mean_aridity_index, soil_sand_content) %>%

title: Multiple Linear Regression: USGS Gage Streamflow
text:
4. Prediction

new_streamflow_data <- tibble(
  mean_annual_precip = c(1972, 994),
  ws_area_sqkm = c(1500, 2),
  mean_baseflow = c(67, 20),
  percent_forested = c(100, 30),
  mean_aridity_index = c(1.1, 1.3),
  soil_sand_content = c(40, 50)
)

predict(streamflow_model, newdata = new_streamflow_data)

       1        2 
796.0836 552.0278

title: Multiple Linear Regression: USGS Gage Streamflow
text:
5. Model Improvements

streamflow_model_log <- lm(log(annual_streamflow_mm) ~ mean_annual_precip +
                                                        ws_area_sqkm +
                                                        mean_baseflow,
                           data = usgs_data)

streamflow_model_sqrt <- lm(sqrt(annual_streamflow_mm) ~ mean_annual_precip +
                                                          ws_area_sqkm,
                            data = usgs_data)

streamflow_model <- lm(annual_streamflow_mm ~ log(mean_annual_precip) +
                                               log(ws_area_sqkm) +
                                               mean_baseflow,
                       data = usgs_data)

streamflow_model_reduced <- lm(annual_streamflow_mm ~ mean_annual_precip +
                                                       ws_area_sqkm,
                               data = usgs_data)

plot(streamflow_model, which = 4)







cooksd <- cooks.distance(streamflow_model)
usgs_filtered <- usgs_data %>% filter(cooksd < 0.5)

streamflow_model_filtered <- lm(annual_streamflow_mm ~ mean_annual_precip +
                                                        ws_area_sqkm,
                                data = usgs_filtered)

streamflow_model_interact <- lm(annual_streamflow_mm ~ mean_annual_precip *
                                                        mean_aridity_index +
                                                        ws_area_sqkm,
                                data = usgs_data)

plot(streamflow_model_log, which = 1)







plot(streamflow_model_log, which = 2)
